educationalPosition: The US-Canada international boundary established in 1846 by the Oregon Treaty bisects traditional cultural territories without the consent of the nations whose lands were divided. The heritage skills pack documents cultural practices as they existed and exist within nations — not as bounded by colonial borders.

borderNotes.crossBorderSituation: Reef net fishing technology and traditional territories span the San Juan Islands (US) and Southern Gulf Islands (Canada). The Point Roberts area is particularly significant as a traditional Lummi fishing ground now in US territory.
borderNotes.sovereigntyNote: Both Lummi (US) and Saanich (Canada) assert cultural relationships with this shared water — the border does not resolve these relationships, it complicates them.

borderNotes.crossBorderSituation: Tlingit peoples occupy coastal Southeast Alaska (US) and northern British Columbia (Canada). Cultural unity predates and supersedes the colonial border.
borderNotes.sovereigntyNote: When discussing Tlingit heritage skills, do not assume US or Canadian jurisdiction defines cultural identity.

borderNotes.crossBorderSituation: Haida traditional territory is Haida Gwaii (Canada) and the southern portion of the Alexander Archipelago, including Old Kasaan (US). The Haida Nation is a single nation whose territory crosses the border.
borderNotes.sovereigntyNote: Haida Nation self-governance assertions apply across the full territory. The colonial boundary does not divide the Haida Nation.

borderNotes.crossBorderSituation: The Columbia River system is shared between nations on both US sides of the river (Oregon and Washington) and represents a significant salmon economy corridor.
borderNotes.sovereigntyNote: Treaty fishing rights on the Columbia River are contested and politically active. The Heritage Skills pack does not adjudicate treaty rights — it documents the cultural heritage.

contentHook.culturalSovereigntyWardenGuidance: When content involves cross-border nations, do not make statements that could be read as privileging one nation's sovereignty claim over another. Present both national contexts where applicable.
